claims.claim_text: Air Canada's chatbot told a bereaved passenger in November 2022 to buy a full-fare ticket and claim a retroactive bereavement refund within 90 days, and Air Canada later refused the refund.
claims.span_hint: section: The Bereavement Fare Disaster, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The underlying tribunal record appears to be the primary source for the incident facts, but CanLII is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: In February 2024, the tribunal ruled against Air Canada, awarded damages and fees, and rejected Air Canada's argument that the chatbot was a separate legal entity.
claims.span_hint: section: The Bereavement Fare Disaster, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The tribunal decision is the right primary source for the ruling details and quoted argument, but it is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The case caused a costly emergency manual review of Air Canada's entire AI infrastructure and severe brand damage.
claims.span_hint: section: The Bereavement Fare Disaster, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Beyond the immediate financial penalty, the global public relations disaster severely damaged their brand reputation and forced a costly, emergency manual review of their entire AI infrastructure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Beyond the tribunal award, the case became a public example of the legal and reputational risk of publishing incorrect chatbot guidance.
claims.rationale: The downstream cost and infrastructure-review specifics are not sourced here and are too precise to keep.

claims.claim_text: Implementing Self-RAG or GraphRAG would have entirely prevented the Air Canada incident.
claims.span_hint: section: The Bereavement Fare Disaster, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: If the engineering team had implemented Self-RAG to critique the generation, or GraphRAG to map the strict dependencies between fare policies, this catastrophic incident would have been entirely prevented.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Additional grounding and answer-verification steps could have reduced the risk of this kind of failure, but no single architecture guarantees prevention.
claims.rationale: This is an absolute counterfactual claim that cannot be honestly sourced from the cited incident.

claims.claim_text: Basic RAG emerged in 2020.
claims.span_hint: section: The Evolution of RAG, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The foundational RAG paper was published in 2020 and is an appropriate primary source for the date.

claims.claim_text: Production RAG systems typically saw a 70/30 success-failure split, illustrated by unnamed medical and legal company failures.
claims.span_hint: section: The Evolution of RAG, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: But as engineering teams deployed RAG to production, they discovered a harsh reality that would repeat across the industry: 70 percent of queries worked flawlessly, but 30 percent failed mysteriously. A medical company found their system missing critical drug interactions because academic literature uses different terminology than a patient's natural language question. A legal tech startup discovered their contract analysis tool could not handle clauses written in dense legalese versus plain English inquiries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: But as engineering teams deployed RAG to production, they discovered that some queries fail because user phrasing and source documents do not line up cleanly. In practice, terminology mismatches and domain-specific language can cause important evidence to be missed.
claims.rationale: The percentages and company anecdotes are unattributed and too specific to retain.

claims.claim_text: These RAG techniques are battle-tested solutions to real production failures.
claims.span_hint: section: The Evolution of RAG, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: What you are learning in this module is not academic theory; it is a collection of battle-tested solutions to real production failures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: What you are learning in this module is a set of patterns designed to address recurring retrieval and grounding failure modes.
claims.rationale: The blanket production-history claim is broader than the evidence provided and should be stated more cautiously.

claims.claim_text: Andrew Ng studied at Stanford, co-founded Coursera, and Coursera relied on Python and React.
claims.span_hint: section: GraphRAG: The Problem with Flat Retrieval, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: But the actual knowledge is spread across a vast corpus: one document mentions "Andrew Ng studied at Stanford," another states "Andrew Ng co-founded Coursera," and a third discusses "Coursera's reliance on Python and React."
claims.suggested_rewrite: But the actual knowledge is spread across a corpus: one document mentions a founder's university, another names the company they founded, and a third describes the technologies that company uses.
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative chain built from real named entities and tech-stack specifics without a source.

claims.claim_text: For larger workloads, GraphRAG implementations often move from in-memory graphs to dedicated graph storage.
claims.span_hint: section: GraphRAG with Neo4j, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: For production workloads, in-memory graphs fail to scale. You must rely on robust graph databases like Neo4j.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For larger workloads, in-memory graphs can become a bottleneck, so teams often move graph storage into a database or service built for larger datasets.
claims.rationale: The original wording is an overgeneralized and vendor-specific prescription.

claims.claim_text: HyDE generates a hypothetical document, embeds it, and uses that embedding to retrieve real documents.
claims.span_hint: section: HyDE: The HyDE Solution
claims.rationale: The HyDE paper abstract directly describes generating a hypothetical document and encoding it for retrieval.

claims.claim_text: GraphRAG builds a graph index from source documents and uses it to answer corpus-level questions.
claims.span_hint: section: GraphRAG: How GraphRAG Works
claims.rationale: The GraphRAG paper abstract directly describes deriving a graph index from source documents for question answering over corpora.

claims.claim_text: Self-RAG uses special reflection tokens to retrieve, generate, and critique through self-reflection.
claims.span_hint: section: Self-RAG, final paragraph before Hybrid Search
claims.rationale: The Self-RAG paper abstract explicitly states that the model uses special reflection tokens for retrieval and self-reflection.

claims.claim_text: Self-RAG is strictly mandatory for high-stakes medical, legal, and financial pipelines.
claims.span_hint: section: The Self-RAG Architecture, sentence after diagram
claims.anchor_text: By adding these critique layers, Self-RAG sacrifices latency for extreme accuracy, making it strictly mandatory for high-stakes medical, legal, and financial pipelines.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By adding these critique layers, Self-RAG increases latency but can be worth the tradeoff in high-stakes domains where grounding matters.
claims.rationale: The universal mandate is opinionated and cannot be supported as a hard factual requirement.

claims.claim_text: HyDE adds 200-500ms and has high quality impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 1 row HyDE
claims.anchor_text: | **HyDE** | Q&A, technical docs | +200-500ms | High |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **HyDE** | Q&A, technical docs | Extra generation step; latency depends on model and infrastructure | Often helpful when query and document phrasing differ |
claims.rationale: The exact latency band is deployment-dependent, but the paper supports the general mechanism and motivation.

claims.claim_text: Hybrid search adds 10-50ms and has medium quality impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 1 row Hybrid Search
claims.anchor_text: | **Hybrid Search** | Mixed queries | +10-50ms | Medium |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Hybrid Search** | Mixed queries | Extra scoring cost depends on the retrieval stack | Often improves robustness on mixed lexical and semantic queries |
claims.rationale: The exact latency number is unsourced here, while the broader lexical-plus-semantic lesson is supportable.

claims.claim_text: GraphRAG adds 100-300ms and has high quality impact for the right use case.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 1 row GraphRAG
claims.anchor_text: | **GraphRAG** | Connected knowledge | +100-300ms | High (for right use case) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **GraphRAG** | Connected knowledge | Graph construction and traversal add overhead; cost depends on implementation | Can help on corpus-level or multi-hop questions |
claims.rationale: The paper supports the use case, but the latency range is not a universal constant.

claims.claim_text: Reranking adds 50-200ms and has high quality impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 1 row Reranking
claims.anchor_text: | **Reranking** | When precision matters | +50-200ms | High |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Reranking** | When precision matters | Second-stage model pass over candidates; cost depends on candidate count and model size | Often improves ranking quality on a shortlisted set |
claims.rationale: The qualitative role is defensible, but the latency figure depends on hardware, model choice, and shortlist size.

claims.claim_text: Self-RAG adds 500-1000ms and has very high quality impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 1 row Self-RAG
claims.anchor_text: | **Self-RAG** | High-stakes applications | +500-1000ms | Very High |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Self-RAG** | High-stakes applications | Multiple critique steps increase latency; impact depends on implementation | Can improve grounding when factuality matters |
claims.rationale: The exact latency claim is benchmark-sensitive, but the general tradeoff is real and paper-backed.

claims.claim_text: HyDE improves recall by 20-40% with 200-500ms latency cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 2 row HyDE
claims.suggested_rewrite: | HyDE | Recall can improve when query and document phrasing differ | Additional generation step adds latency |
claims.rationale: The paper supports improvement over baseline methods, but these specific ranges are not a universal benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Hybrid search improves precision by 15-25% with 10-50ms latency cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 2 row Hybrid Search
claims.anchor_text: | Hybrid Search | +15-25% precision | +10-50ms |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Hybrid Search | Precision often improves on mixed lexical and semantic queries | Extra scoring cost depends on the retrieval stack |
claims.rationale: These numeric gains are highly workload-dependent and need benchmark context to keep exact.

claims.claim_text: Reranking improves precision by 30-50% with 50-200ms latency cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 2 row Reranking
claims.anchor_text: | Reranking | +30-50% precision | +50-200ms |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Reranking | Precision often improves on a reranked shortlist | Added model pass over candidates |
claims.rationale: The exact improvement range is benchmark-specific rather than a general fact.

claims.claim_text: Self-RAG improves accuracy by 10-15% with 500-1000ms latency cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 2 row Self-RAG
claims.anchor_text: | Self-RAG | +10-15% accuracy | +500-1000ms |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Self-RAG | Accuracy and factuality can improve when critique loops help | Multiple critique steps add noticeable latency |
claims.rationale: The paper supports quality gains, but the exact percentages and latency band are deployment-specific.

claims.claim_text: GraphRAG improves complex-query performance by 25-40% with 100-300ms latency cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Pattern Selection and Economics, table 2 row GraphRAG
claims.anchor_text: | GraphRAG | +25-40% for complex queries | +100-300ms |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | GraphRAG | Can help on complex multi-hop or corpus-level questions | Graph construction and traversal add overhead |
claims.rationale: The GraphRAG paper supports the general benefit on global questions, but these exact ranges are not universal.

claims.claim_text: HyDE was accidentally discovered at Carnegie Mellon in December 2022 when a graduate student fed generated summaries into the index, improving recall by 20 to 40 percent.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. In December 2022, researchers at Carnegie Mellon University accidentally discovered HyDE when a graduate student mistakenly fed the LLM's generated summaries into their search index instead of the raw queries, radically improving recall by 20 to 40 percent.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. HyDE was introduced in 2022 as a retrieval method that generates a hypothetical document before dense search.
claims.rationale: The discovery anecdote and improvement numbers are too specific and are not supported by the primary paper.

claims.claim_text: Stephen Robertson published BM25 in 1994, and 32 years later it remains the foundational baseline algorithm for Elasticsearch and Apache Solr.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.anchor_text: 2. Stephen Robertson published the BM25 lexical ranking algorithm in 1994. More than 32 years later, it remains the foundational baseline algorithm for Elasticsearch and Apache Solr.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. BM25 has been a core lexical ranking method in information retrieval for decades and remains a common baseline in search systems.
claims.rationale: The product-specific claim is only partially allowlisted, and the dated arithmetic plus superlative wording should be softened.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft processed over 500,000 Enron emails with GraphRAG in April 2024 and surfaced hidden communication clusters missed for two decades.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. In April 2024, Microsoft explicitly demonstrated the power of GraphRAG by processing over 500,000 Enron emails, successfully mapping out hidden corporate communication clusters that financial investigators had entirely missed for two decades.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. GraphRAG has been demonstrated on large text corpora to surface clusters and relationships that are hard to recover with flat retrieval alone.
claims.rationale: This named-case anecdote bundles multiple precise claims that are not grounded by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: Cross-encoders can be up to 1000 times slower than bi-encoders, and the two-stage retrieval pipeline was introduced by Facebook AI in 2019 as a permanent industry requirement.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. Because cross-encoder models evaluate pairs simultaneously, they operate up to 1000 times slower than bi-encoders during large-scale retrieval scoring, cementing the two-stage retrieval pipeline introduced by Facebook AI in 2019 as a permanent industry requirement.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. Because cross-encoders score query-document pairs jointly, they are far slower than bi-encoders at large retrieval scales and are usually used as a second-stage reranker.
claims.rationale: The general speed tradeoff is real, but the exact factor, attribution, and permanence claim are too specific as written.

further_reading.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
further_reading.why_relevant: Foundational RAG paper for the baseline architecture this module extends.

further_reading.title: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary HyDE paper describing hypothetical document embeddings for zero-shot retrieval.

further_reading.title: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary GraphRAG paper for graph-indexed retrieval and corpus-level question answering.

further_reading.title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary paper for retrieval-time self-reflection and reflection-token control in RAG systems.

notes: No shared section pool was assumed. The Air Canada tribunal appears citable only through off-allowlist CanLII material, and the two economics tables plus Did You Know bullets contain multiple benchmark/anecdotal claims that are better rewritten than force-cited.
